title: Dunkirk Evacuation (Operation Dynamo)
summary: Operation Dynamo was the Allied evacuation from Dunkirk following the German breakthrough at Sedan. Over nine days, 338,226 Allied soldiers were lifted from the beaches and harbour by a fleet of warships and civilian vessels — though all heavy equipment was abandoned.
context: By 20 May 1940 German Panzer forces had reached the Channel coast, cutting off the British Expeditionary Force and French First Army from the main French forces to the south. Surrounded on three sides, with the sea at their backs, the Allied armies faced destruction or surrender. The 'Halt Order' of 23–26 May — attributed to Hitler and supported by Rundstedt — paused the Panzer advance short of the Dunkirk perimeter, citing concerns about the terrain (canals, soft ground unsuitable for tanks) and Göring's promise that the Luftwaffe could destroy the trapped forces alone. The Luftwaffe's failure to carry out this promise, combined with cloud cover and RAF fighter cover, enabled Vice Admiral Ramsay to organise the largest evacuation in British history. Some 861 vessels — including 228 private civilian 'little ships' — participated. Churchill had privately hoped for 45,000 evacuations; the final figure of 338,226 was described as a 'miracle'. The evacuation preserved the British Army as a fighting force but left France without effective Allied support.

axis.strength: Army Group A Panzers encircling the perimeter; Luftwaffe

key_event: The 'Halt Order' of 23–26 May paused the Panzer advance short of Dunkirk — whether deliberate or operational caution, it allowed the evacuation to proceed. 338,226 troops evacuated; 35,000–40,000 French troops captured. All Allied heavy equipment abandoned.
outcome: Allied strategic success despite tactical defeat. 338,226 troops evacuated including 139,000 French. All heavy equipment (475 tanks, 1,000 guns, 35,000 vehicles) abandoned. Churchill cautioned that 'wars are not won by evacuations.'

casualties.allied: ~68,000 killed or captured; 243 ships sunk; 177 RAF aircraft lost

sources.ref: National Archives (UK). ADM 199/786: Operation Dynamo — Naval Commander's Despatch, June 1940.
Divine, David. The Nine Days of Dunkirk. Faber and Faber, 1959.
sources.note: Classic account based on naval records and participant interviews.

sources.ref: Sebag-Montefiore, Hugh. Dunkirk: Fight to the Last Man. Viking, 2006.
sources.note: Comprehensive modern account drawing on previously sealed British records.

sources.ref: Blaxland, Gregory. Destination Dunkirk: The Story of Gort's Army. Kimber, 1973.
